evidence_records.raw_text:
| Time step | O&M sample text |
|---|---|
| T1 | Begin the leak map on Aurex E-Motion-412 LR by recording battery coolant level, residue color, undertray wetness, recent thermal-system repair history, and whether seepage is already visible at the left coolant-plate outlet while the pack remains in the as-received condition with no covers moved. |
| T2 | Power the vehicle down, isolate the high-voltage pack, and allow the local cooling branch to equalize. Then remove the rear underbody shield and the left pack-side splash cover while keeping the hose angle unchanged so the original quick-coupler seating witness can still be read. |
| T3 | Wipe only the loose surface film first, leave the wet edge untouched, and trace the leak path from the coolant-plate seam toward the outlet hose, the quick-coupler lock window, the O-ring seat, and the nearby bracket drain edge so the actual origin is not blurred by cleaning too early. |
| T4 | Install the local pressure tool on the coolant-plate inlet and outlet, cap the neighboring branch, and hold the specified pressure long enough to observe whether fresh wetting begins at the plate seam, the coupler body, the hose clamp line, or the tube insertion stop inside the connector. |
| T5 | Compare the suspect joint with the opposite coolant-plate branch, paying attention to hose twist, clamp clocking, connector latch height, tube insertion depth, and any side-load mark where the support bracket may have forced the plastic coupler body off-axis. This comparison should distinguish a deformed connector body from a hose-routing load or a clamp-position error before the joint is opened. |
| T6 | Inspect the aluminum plate edge, the molded quick connector, the green O-ring land, the hose end, and the nearby harness clip for dried coolant crystals, abrasion, plastic stress whitening, or metal staining that would separate connector leakage from a plate-side crack. |
| T7 | Mark the confirmed leak boundary directly at the leaking hardware, photograph the coolant-plate seam and the quick-coupler seat from more than one angle, and note clearly whether the failure belongs to the plate seam, the O-ring interface, the connector shell, or the hose-routing condition. |
| T8 | Refit only the access parts needed to protect the opened area, leave the identified leak point readable for downstream repair, and close the record with the exact hardware named as battery coolant-plate outlet quick connector or coolant-plate seam instead of leaving a generic leak description. The accepted leak map should still let the next repair start from one named outlet-side boundary rather than from a broad pack-coolant leak note. |

task.label: T1
task.surface_form: Begin the leak map on Aurex E-Motion-412 LR by recording battery coolant level, residue color, undertray wetness, recent thermal-system repair history, and whether seepage is already visible at the left coolant-plate outlet while the pack remains in the as-received condition with no covers moved.

task.label: T2
task.surface_form: Power the vehicle down, isolate the high-voltage pack, and allow the local cooling branch to equalize. Then remove the rear underbody shield and the left pack-side splash cover while keeping the hose angle unchanged so the original quick-coupler seating witness can still be read.

task.label: T3
task.surface_form: Wipe only the loose surface film first, leave the wet edge untouched, and trace the leak path from the coolant-plate seam toward the outlet hose, the quick-coupler lock window, the O-ring seat, and the nearby bracket drain edge so the actual origin is not blurred by cleaning too early.

concept_mentions.label: quick-coupler lock window
concept_mentions.description: lock window on quick coupler component

task.label: T4
task.surface_form: Install the local pressure tool on the coolant-plate inlet and outlet, cap the neighboring branch, and hold the specified pressure long enough to observe whether fresh wetting begins at the plate seam, the coupler body, the hose clamp line, or the tube insertion stop inside the connector.

task.label: T5
task.surface_form: Compare the suspect joint with the opposite coolant-plate branch, paying attention to hose twist, clamp clocking, connector latch height, tube insertion depth, and any side-load mark where the support bracket may have forced the plastic coupler body off-axis. This comparison should distinguish a deformed connector body from a hose-routing load or a clamp-position error before the joint is opened.

task.label: T6
task.surface_form: Inspect the aluminum plate edge, the molded quick connector, the green O-ring land, the hose end, and the nearby harness clip for dried coolant crystals, abrasion, plastic stress whitening, or metal staining that would separate connector leakage from a plate-side crack.

task.label: T7
task.surface_form: Mark the confirmed leak boundary directly at the leaking hardware, photograph the coolant-plate seam and the quick-coupler seat from more than one angle, and note clearly whether the failure belongs to the plate seam, the O-ring interface, the connector shell, or the hose-routing condition.

task.label: T8
task.surface_form: Refit only the access parts needed to protect the opened area, leave the identified leak point readable for downstream repair, and close the record with the exact hardware named as battery coolant-plate outlet quick connector or coolant-plate seam instead of leaving a generic leak description. The accepted leak map should still let the next repair start from one named outlet-side boundary rather than from a broad pack-coolant leak note.

evidence_records.raw_text:
| Time step | O&M sample text |
|---|---|
| T1 | At Velorian PackLine-287 AWD, reproduce the service-disconnect seating complaint by noting latch feel, insertion force, handle flushness, any HVIL warning, and whether the orange handle sits proud of the battery access opening or snaps back after seating. Keep the opening complaint tied to the actual handle position and HVIL response so later seat checks stay on the same disconnect boundary. |
| T2 | Depower the pack, verify zero residual high-voltage potential at the service-disconnect cavity, and remove the access lid so the disconnect plug, blade set, guide rail, interlock pins, latch window, and surrounding sealing edge are all visible in one service view. |
| T3 | Pull the service disconnect straight out without rotating it, then inspect the male blades, female socket faces, guide ribs, CPA feature, handle pivot, and latch hook for burrs, arc marks, plastic rollover, uneven insertion witness lines, or debris in the guide channel. |
| T4 | Compare the suspect disconnect with a known-good reference or spare, checking seating depth, latch travel, blade exposure, guide-rib alignment, and the shoulder position of the interlock pins so the seat issue is tied to geometry rather than to feel alone. |
| T5 | Rework the seat condition by correcting the misaligned guide feature, removing only the minor burr or displaced plastic that blocks full travel, and cleaning the contact area without changing the original blade profile, terminal tension, or latch geometry. The correction has to preserve the blade path and the interlock-pin relationship rather than forcing the handle closed by cosmetic trimming. |
| T6 | Reinstall the service disconnect in one continuous motion, confirm full blade entry, verify the latch hook snaps fully into the window, and check that the orange handle now remains flush with the cover plane instead of rebounding or sitting with a visible gap. |
| T7 | Restore the service lid, wake the pack, and confirm through two seat checks that the HVIL path, service-disconnect status, and ready-state permissive remain stable with no handle movement, no latch relaxation, and no repeat interlock warning. The release condition is a disconnect that seats flush and keeps the HVIL path stable after the lid and surrounding trim return to final position. |

task.label: T1
task.surface_form: At Velorian PackLine-287 AWD, reproduce the service-disconnect seating complaint by noting latch feel, insertion force, handle flushness, any HVIL warning, and whether the orange handle sits proud of the battery access opening or snaps back after seating. Keep the opening complaint tied to the actual handle position and HVIL response so later seat checks stay on the same disconnect boundary.

task.label: T2
task.surface_form: Depower the pack, verify zero residual high-voltage potential at the service-disconnect cavity, and remove the access lid so the disconnect plug, blade set, guide rail, interlock pins, latch window, and surrounding sealing edge are all visible in one service view.

task.label: T3
task.surface_form: Pull the service disconnect straight out without rotating it, then inspect the male blades, female socket faces, guide ribs, CPA feature, handle pivot, and latch hook for burrs, arc marks, plastic rollover, uneven insertion witness lines, or debris in the guide channel.

task.label: T4
task.surface_form: Compare the suspect disconnect with a known-good reference or spare, checking seating depth, latch travel, blade exposure, guide-rib alignment, and the shoulder position of the interlock pins so the seat issue is tied to geometry rather than to feel alone.

task.label: T5
task.surface_form: Rework the seat condition by correcting the misaligned guide feature, removing only the minor burr or displaced plastic that blocks full travel, and cleaning the contact area without changing the original blade profile, terminal tension, or latch geometry. The correction has to preserve the blade path and the interlock-pin relationship rather than forcing the handle closed by cosmetic trimming.

task.label: T6
task.surface_form: Reinstall the service disconnect in one continuous motion, confirm full blade entry, verify the latch hook snaps fully into the window, and check that the orange handle now remains flush with the cover plane instead of rebounding or sitting with a visible gap.

concept_mentions.label: handle flush with cover plane
concept_mentions.description: state of handle flush with cover plane

concept_mentions.label: latch hook fully snapped
concept_mentions.description: state of latch hook fully snapped

task.label: T7
task.surface_form: Restore the service lid, wake the pack, and confirm through two seat checks that the HVIL path, service-disconnect status, and ready-state permissive remain stable with no handle movement, no latch relaxation, and no repeat interlock warning. The release condition is a disconnect that seats flush and keeps the HVIL path stable after the lid and surrounding trim return to final position.

evidence_records.raw_text:
| Time step | O&M sample text |
|---|---|
| T1 | Start the HV output corrosion review on Helion E-Motion-536 Plus by noting whether corrosion was found during scheduled service or after a moisture complaint, and capture the as-found condition of the HV output boot, stud cover, cable tray, and surrounding pack wall before any hardware is loosened. |
| T2 | Isolate the pack, remove the HV output stud cover, and expose the copper cable lug, terminal nut, washer stack, plated stud thread, insulating boot, and stud shoulder without changing the cable angle more than necessary. Keep the cable angle and boot clocking readable so the later moisture-path check is tied to the true as-found geometry. |
| T3 | Inspect the corrosion pattern across the terminal nut flats, lug face, stud thread, washer stack, boot interior, and nearby insulator surface to determine whether the source is trapped water, road contamination, galvanic attack, or reduced clamp load. This pattern review should separate clamp-loss corrosion at the contact stack from moisture trapped under the boot or runoff entering from outside the pocket. |
| T4 | Remove the nut and washers in sequence, lay the hardware out by order, and examine the underside of the cable lug, the stud seating land, and the first thread turns for pitting, oxide buildup, contact shadowing, or heat tint beneath the contact face. |
| T5 | Measure contact resistance from the cable lug to the stud and compare it with the opposite-side output connection, taking care that the probe points land on clean metal and not on corroded thread, coated washer faces, or discolored nut surfaces. |
| T6 | Inspect the rubber boot lip, the drain path around the cover edge, the cable-entry orientation, and the nearby harness support to see whether water tracked down the cable, bypassed the boot seal, or sat trapped at the bottom of the stud pocket. |
| T7 | If the joint is under inspection only, reassemble the washer stack in the documented order and restore the insulating boot with the cable held at its natural exit angle. If pitting, thread damage, or heat history exceeds reuse limits, tag the exact stud, lug, washer, or nut for replacement and name that failed hardware explicitly in the record. |
| T8 | Close the stud pocket and restore the cover only after the record includes the corrosion location, corrosion severity, hardware order, moisture path assessment, and measured resistance comparison to the healthy reference output joint. The release note should still let the next technician identify the exact stud stack, corrosion source, and moisture path without reopening the pocket to rediscover the boundary. |

task.label: T1
task.surface_form: Start the HV output corrosion review on Helion E-Motion-536 Plus by noting whether corrosion was found during scheduled service or after a moisture complaint, and capture the as-found condition of the HV output boot, stud cover, cable tray, and surrounding pack wall before any hardware is loosened.

task.label: T2
task.surface_form: Isolate the pack, remove the HV output stud cover, and expose the copper cable lug, terminal nut, washer stack, plated stud thread, insulating boot, and stud shoulder without changing the cable angle more than necessary. Keep the cable angle and boot clocking readable so the later moisture-path check is tied to the true as-found geometry.

task.label: T3
task.surface_form: Inspect the corrosion pattern across the terminal nut flats, lug face, stud thread, washer stack, boot interior, and nearby insulator surface to determine whether the source is trapped water, road contamination, galvanic attack, or reduced clamp load. This pattern review should separate clamp-loss corrosion at the contact stack from moisture trapped under the boot or runoff entering from outside the pocket.

task.label: T4
task.surface_form: Remove the nut and washers in sequence, lay the hardware out by order, and examine the underside of the cable lug, the stud seating land, and the first thread turns for pitting, oxide buildup, contact shadowing, or heat tint beneath the contact face.

task.label: T5
task.surface_form: Measure contact resistance from the cable lug to the stud and compare it with the opposite-side output connection, taking care that the probe points land on clean metal and not on corroded thread, coated washer faces, or discolored nut surfaces.

task.label: T6
task.surface_form: Inspect the rubber boot lip, the drain path around the cover edge, the cable-entry orientation, and the nearby harness support to see whether water tracked down the cable, bypassed the boot seal, or sat trapped at the bottom of the stud pocket.

task.label: T7
task.surface_form: If the joint is under inspection only, reassemble the washer stack in the documented order and restore the insulating boot with the cable held at its natural exit angle. If pitting, thread damage, or heat history exceeds reuse limits, tag the exact stud, lug, washer, or nut for replacement and name that failed hardware explicitly in the record.

task.label: T8
task.surface_form: Close the stud pocket and restore the cover only after the record includes the corrosion location, corrosion severity, hardware order, moisture path assessment, and measured resistance comparison to the healthy reference output joint. The release note should still let the next technician identify the exact stud stack, corrosion source, and moisture path without reopening the pocket to rediscover the boundary.
